_about: ICP for the *recipient* of the outbound (the buyer Maya at Anchored is targeting), not the sender. Anchored is a fictional cloud-runtime / ITDR vendor; its buyers are security leaders at mid-market and enterprise SaaS companies.
industry: Mid-market SaaS (target customer of a cloud-runtime / ITDR vendor)

priorities: ship measurable reduction in mean-time-to-detect for production runtime threats
stand up identity-threat-detection coverage before the next SOC2 / ISO 27001 audit
rationalise the runtime-security stack down from 4 vendors to 2 without losing coverage
keep the SOC tier-1 alert backlog under 200 open at end-of-shift
painPoints: rule-decay loop: detection rules go stale faster than the threat actor playbook moves; stale rules ship as alert volume against tier-1 analysts who are already running hot, and the cost shows up in tier-1 attrition two quarters later
alert-fatigue blast radius: 60-70% of tier-1 alerts close as no-action; the analyst hours go to confirming benign traffic instead of chasing a real lateral-movement pattern

tooling-stack overlap: EDR + CSPM + CWPP + ITDR vendors each surface the same containers from a different angle; the team cannot point to a canonical dashboard when the same exec-server CVE shows up in three of them on the same Monday
audit-board pressure: the board asks one question after a Capital One-style breach in the news ('would we catch that?') and the answer involves stitching exports from four vendor consoles by Friday

priorities: report a breach-readiness score the board recognises without footnotes every quarter
consolidate cyber spend by 15-25% in next-year budget without dropping coverage
retain the SOC team through Q4 (named replacement risk for two senior analysts)
land the next SOC2 Type II audit with zero exceptions
painPoints: board-question latency: 'are we exposed to the SolarWinds-style supply-chain risk in the news?' lands Friday; the answer takes the team until Tuesday and lives in a sharepoint doc nobody opens again
vendor-sprawl ROI: cyber budget grew 18% YoY and the breach-readiness score did not; the CFO asks why at every quarterly business review
tier-1 burnout signal: 30%+ analyst attrition in 12 months; the runbook lives in someone's head, not in the SIEM
audit-trail gaps: SOC2 auditor asks 'who approved this exception in production' and the answer involves a junior analyst stitching screenshots from three vendor consoles at midnight
